reasoning: In (1), the person being scolded (Vassar) is the one who did the wrong action; thus “she elamped …” refers to Vassar.
Still in (1), “her plumbus” is the victim’s/subject’s item; Smith scolds Vassar because Vassar damaged Smith’s plumbus.
In (4), with “even though,” the difficulty belongs to the subject; thus the plumbus that elamped was Smith’s.
In (5), the failure was due to being ‘too efrimious’; given the size clue, the ricktick was too large to schleem Radcliffe’s lapsine.
In (7), Vassar scolds Holyoke because Holyoke elamped the plumbus.
In (9), having a riplin explains earlier arrival; thus “she had a riplin” refers to Vassar.
In (11), the first “it” is the item doing the schleeming: Barnard’s ricktick schleemed the lapsine.
In (11), the second “it” is the item being schleemed: Wellesley’s lapsine.

‘Ravint’ = before and ‘bevint’ = after. (12) Barnard arrived before everyone; (4) Smith before Wellesley; (10) Vassar after Smith; (11) Wellesley after Vassar; (6) Radcliffe after everyone who arrived; (3) Holyoke did not arrive.
